past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 9 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with oral agents
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 3 years ago
family_history: Father died of lung cancer at age 70
Mother had a stroke at age 79
Sister with breast cancer at age 55, in remission
No family history of brain tumors or neurological disorders

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 20 pack-year history, quit 15 years ago
social_history.occupation: High school math teacher, currently on medical leave
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story house
social_history.exercise: Used to walk 3 miles daily, stopped 3 weeks ago due to worsening symptoms

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place but occasionally confused about exact date. Mildly inattentive, makes errors on serial 7s after the second subtraction. Speech is fluent but with notable word-finding pauses and occasional paraphasic errors. Can follow two-step commands but struggles with three-step. Short-term memory impaired: recalls 1 out of 3 objects at 5 minutes. Long-term memory grossly intact.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3.5 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation on the right; left homonymous hemianopia detected on confrontation testing. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Mild left facial droop noted on smile, forehead sparing. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper and lower extremities: 5/5 strength throughout. Left upper extremity: 4/5 deltoid, 4/5 biceps, 4-/5 wrist extension, 4/5 grip, 4/5 finger abduction. Left lower extremity: 4+/5 hip flexion, 5/5 knee extension, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion. No pronator drift on the right; subtle left pronator drift present.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. Proprioception intact in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 3+. Left plantar response equivocal, borderline extensor. Right plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Right finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin normal. Left finger-to-nose with mild dysmetria. Left heel-to-shin mildly impaired.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulatory with mildly wide-based gait. Left leg circumduction noted. Tandem gait impaired.
neurological_exam.additional: Papilledema noted on fundoscopic exam bilaterally, more prominent on the right. No meningismus. No asterixis.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive headaches, left arm weakness, and a new-onset seizure
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Robert Caldwell is a 61-year-old right-handed man and high school math teacher who presents to the emergency department after experiencing a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home approximately 2 hours ago. His wife reports that the seizure lasted roughly 90 seconds and was preceded by his left arm stiffening and shaking for about 15 seconds before becoming generalized. He was post-ictal and confused for approximately 20 minutes before regaining conversational ability, although his wife notes he has still not returned to his baseline. There was no tongue bite or urinary incontinence. He has no prior history of seizures. On further questioning, his wife describes a 3-week history of progressively worsening headaches, initially attributed by their primary care physician to tension-type headaches and treated with ibuprofen. These headaches are worst in the morning upon waking, are dull and pressure-like, and have been accompanied by nausea on several occasions, with two episodes of vomiting over the past week. She also reports that over the past 2 weeks, he has developed noticeable weakness of his left arm, initially manifesting as difficulty buttoning his shirt and clumsiness when writing on the blackboard with his left hand for emphasis. His colleagues at school remarked that he seemed to be struggling to find the right words during his lectures, something entirely out of character for a man known for his articulate teaching style. Over the past week, his wife has noticed he seems more forgetful and occasionally confused, once becoming disoriented briefly while driving on a familiar route home from school. He was placed on medical leave from teaching five days ago after an episode where he lost his train of thought mid-sentence during a parent-teacher conference. He denies recent fever, chills, night sweats, or significant weight loss, although his wife thinks he may have lost a few pounds over the past month due to decreased appetite from the nausea.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz alpha activity, mildly slowed from expected 9-10 Hz
background.symmetry: Asymmetric; right hemisphere shows more prominent slowing and amplitude attenuation compared to the left
background.reactivity: PDR attenuates with eye opening bilaterally

findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with underlying structural lesion in the right frontotemporal region
May represent cortical irritability in peri-lesional cortex; raises concern for epileptogenicity

findings.morphology: Phase-reversing sharp waves with aftergoing slow waves
findings.clinical_correlation: Epileptiform discharges consistent with peri-tumoral cortical irritability and seizure risk

findings.morphology: Reduced amplitude and slowed frequencies over the right hemisphere
findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with right hemispheric structural pathology causing regional cortical dysfunction

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, does not significantly obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to clinical concern for elevated intracranial pressure
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response. Normal photic driving at 10-14 Hz on the left; absent photic driving on the right.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG. Continuous right frontotemporal polymorphic delta activity with intermittent rhythmic delta and occasional epileptiform sharp waves is consistent with a structural lesion in the right frontotemporal region with associated cortical irritability. Diffuse right hemispheric slowing and amplitude attenuation suggest widespread regional dysfunction. Clinical correlation with neuroimaging is strongly recommended.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to clinical concern for elevated ICP.
recommended_actions: Urgent brain MRI with and without contrast
Consider initiation of antiepileptic medication given epileptiform discharges and clinical seizure
Continuous EEG monitoring if subclinical seizures are suspected

findings.type: Heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis
findings.location: Right frontal lobe, centered in the right precentral and middle frontal gyri, extending into the right insular cortex and right basal ganglia

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense with areas of intrinsic T1 shortening suggesting hemorrhage or proteinaceous material
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous signal with mixed hyper- and hypointensity; central necrotic cavity is markedly T2 hyperintense
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive perilesional hyperintensity extending throughout the right frontal white matter and into the right temporal lobe and right corona radiata, representing vasogenic edema
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion along the enhancing rim consistent with high cellularity; central necrotic region does not show restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Low ADC values at the enhancing periphery confirming true restriction; high ADC in the necrotic center
signal_characteristics.Post_contrast: Thick, irregular ring enhancement with nodular components along the medial and posterior margins; central non-enhancing necrotic core

findings.mass_effect: Significant rightward-to-leftward mass effect with 8 mm leftward midline shift at the septum pellucidum. Compression of the right lateral ventricle with early trapping of the right temporal horn. Effacement of the right-sided cortical sulci. Mild uncal herniation on the right.
findings.borders: Irregular, infiltrative margins with poorly defined borders, especially along the medial and posterior aspects where tumor extends along white matter tracts

findings.location: Right frontal, temporal, and parietal white matter
findings.size: Extensive, spanning approximately 8 cm in greatest dimension

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Diffuse hyperintensity in right hemispheric white matter
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal tracking along white matter

findings.mass_effect: Contributing to the overall mass effect and midline shift
findings.borders: Extends well beyond the enhancing tumor margins, finger-like projections along white matter tracts

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified in the brain to suggest multifocal disease or metastasis
No leptomeningeal enhancement to suggest CSF dissemination
Right lateral ventricle is compressed; left lateral ventricle is mildly dilated suggesting early obstructive component
Normal flow voids in the major intracranial arteries on MRA
mri.impression: 1. Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontal mass (5.8 x 4.3 x 4.6 cm) with central necrosis, irregular ring enhancement, extensive surrounding vasogenic edema, and 8 mm leftward midline shift. Imaging features are highly characteristic of high-grade glioma (WHO grade IV glioblastoma). 2. Restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim consistent with high cellularity. 3. Significant mass effect with early right uncal herniation. 4. No evidence of multifocal disease or leptomeningeal spread. Neurosurgical consultation is recommended urgently.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Thick irregular ring enhancement with central necrosis, infiltrative borders, perilesional edema out of proportion to enhancing volume, restricted diffusion at rim
Solitary lesion without known primary malignancy; metastases tend to be more spherical with thinner, more uniform enhancement and are located at the gray-white junction
No fever or systemic infection; abscess shows smooth thin ring enhancement and central restricted diffusion from pus, unlike the peripheral restriction seen here
Lymphoma typically shows homogeneous enhancement without necrosis and is often periventricular; patient is immunocompetent

recommended_actions: Urgent neurosurgical consultation for surgical planning
Consider initiation of dexamethasone for cerebral edema and mass effect
MRI perfusion and MR spectroscopy may be obtained to further characterize the lesion if needed for surgical planning
Stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection for tissue diagnosis and molecular profiling

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with known type 2 diabetes and possible stress response in the setting of seizure and intracranial pathology

Coagulation.clinical_significance: Normal coagulation, acceptable for surgical intervention

Other.clinical_significance: Moderately controlled diabetes; baseline for chemotherapy planning

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are largely within normal limits. CBC, coagulation studies, and hepatic and renal function are normal, establishing adequate baseline parameters for potential surgical intervention and future chemotherapy. Glucose is mildly elevated consistent with known diabetes, and HbA1c of 7.2% confirms moderately controlled diabetes. No evidence of systemic infection or significant metabolic derangement. LDH is within normal range.

findings.location: Right frontal enhancing rim of the mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Markedly elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV ratio 4.8 compared to contralateral normal-appearing white matter)
signal_characteristics.rCBF: Elevated relative cerebral blood flow in the enhancing periphery
signal_characteristics.Ktrans: Elevated permeability in the enhancing rim consistent with blood-brain barrier breakdown

findings.location: Central necrotic core of the mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Markedly reduced rCBV in the central non-enhancing region, consistent with necrosis

additional_observations: The elevated rCBV pattern in the enhancing rim is characteristic of neovascularity seen in high-grade gliomas
No elevated perfusion in the surrounding FLAIR abnormality zone, suggesting primarily vasogenic edema rather than infiltrating tumor in the edematous region
Perfusion characteristics help distinguish from brain abscess (which typically shows low rCBV in the enhancing rim) and low-grade glioma (which shows lower rCBV values)
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates markedly elevated rCBV (ratio 4.8) in the enhancing rim of the right frontal mass, strongly supporting a diagnosis of high-grade glioma. The central necrotic region shows absent perfusion. These findings are characteristic of glioblastoma and help distinguish from brain abscess or lower-grade glioma.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: rCBV ratio >4.0 in enhancing rim is highly characteristic of glioblastoma
Brain abscess typically shows reduced rCBV in the enhancing rim, unlike the elevated values seen here

recommended_actions: Proceed with MR spectroscopy for additional metabolic characterization
Neurosurgical consultation for maximal safe resection
Perfusion hot spot can guide stereotactic biopsy target if open resection is not feasible

findings.location: Right frontal enhancing mass, voxel placed over the enhancing rim

signal_characteristics.NAA: Markedly reduced (NAA/Cr ratio 0.4, normal >1.6), consistent with neuronal loss or displacement
signal_characteristics.Lipid_Lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks present, indicating necrosis and anaerobic metabolism

additional_observations: The spectral pattern of elevated choline, reduced NAA, and prominent lipid/lactate is characteristic of high-grade glioma
The markedly elevated Cho/NAA ratio (9.5) is strongly associated with WHO grade IV glioma
Perilesional FLAIR region shows mildly elevated choline (Cho/Cr 1.4), suggesting possible microscopic tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing margin
output.impression: MR spectroscopy of the right frontal mass demonstrates a metabolic profile highly characteristic of high-grade glioma: markedly elevated choline (reflecting high membrane turnover and cellularity), severely reduced NAA (reflecting neuronal destruction), and prominent lipid and lactate peaks (reflecting necrosis and hypoxia). The Cho/NAA ratio of 9.5 is strongly associated with glioblastoma (WHO grade IV).

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Cho/NAA ratio >2.0 with lipid/lactate peaks is highly characteristic
Low-grade gliomas typically show mild choline elevation without lipid/lactate peaks and higher NAA

recommended_actions: Tissue diagnosis via stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection remains the gold standard
Spectroscopic findings support the need for aggressive surgical and adjuvant treatment planning

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.clinical_significance: No evidence of primary lung malignancy

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: Normal size and enhancement bilaterally, no masses
Degenerative changes in lumbar spine, no lytic or blastic lesions

output.interpretation: CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast is unremarkable for primary malignancy or metastatic disease. Lungs are clear without masses, nodules, or lymphadenopathy. No hepatic, renal, adrenal, or osseous lesions. Mildly enlarged prostate consistent with known BPH. These findings support a primary brain tumor rather than brain metastasis from an occult systemic malignancy.

Histopathology.clinical_significance: Confirms high-grade malignant glioma, the most aggressive primary brain tumor
High cellularity with pleomorphic astrocytic cells

Molecular Markers.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype status; associated with worse prognosis compared to IDH-mutant gliomas
Unmethylated MGMT is associated with reduced response to temozolomide chemotherapy; however, current standard of care still includes temozolomide
Expected in glioblastoma; codeletion is characteristic of oligodendroglioma
TERT promoter mutation with IDH-wildtype supports glioblastoma diagnosis per WHO 2021 criteria
EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype GBM; may be relevant for targeted therapy trials
High proliferative index consistent with aggressive tumor biology

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy confirms glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV. Histopathology demonstrates the hallmark features of glioblastoma including markedly increased cellularity, pseudopalisading necrosis, and microvascular proliferation. Molecular profiling reveals IDH-wildtype status, unmethylated MGMT promoter, TERT promoter mutation, and EGFR amplification. The combination of IDH-wildtype status with TERT promoter mutation satisfies the WHO 2021 criteria for glioblastoma even in the absence of classic histological features. MGMT unmethylated status suggests relatively lower benefit from temozolomide, though it remains part of standard therapy. Ki-67 of 35% reflects high proliferative activity.
abnormal_values_summary: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV confirmed
MGMT promoter: unmethylated (less favorable for temozolomide response)

findings.location: Left hemisphere (Broca's area in left inferior frontal gyrus and Wernicke's area in left posterior superior temporal gyrus)

signal_characteristics.Language_activation: Robust left-lateralized language activation. Language laterality index 0.82 (strongly left-dominant).
signal_characteristics.Proximity_to_tumor: Language areas are contralateral to the tumor; no significant activation within or immediately adjacent to the tumor

findings.location: Bilateral primary motor cortex along precentral gyri

signal_characteristics.Left_hand_motor: Left hand motor activation maps to the right precentral gyrus, located along the posterior-superior margin of the tumor. Activation is displaced posteriorly and superiorly by the mass.
signal_characteristics.Right_hand_motor: Right hand motor activation maps to the left precentral gyrus, normal location.
signal_characteristics.Foot_motor: Bilateral foot motor activation along the medial precentral gyri, right-sided activation near the superior margin of the tumor

findings.mass_effect: Tumor displaces the right precentral gyrus motor cortex posteriorly and superiorly

additional_observations: The left hand motor representation is immediately adjacent to the posterior-superior margin of the tumor, placing it at risk during surgical resection
The distance from the tumor enhancing margin to the nearest motor activation cluster is approximately 6 mm
DTI tractography shows displacement but not disruption of the right corticospinal tract along the posterior margin of the tumor
Supplementary motor area activation is preserved bilaterally
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates that language areas are left-lateralized and contralateral to the right frontal tumor, posing low risk for language deficit from surgery. However, left hand/arm motor representation in the right precentral gyrus is immediately adjacent to the posterior-superior margin of the tumor (approximately 6 mm from the enhancing edge). The right corticospinal tract is displaced but not disrupted. Intraoperative cortical stimulation mapping and awake craniotomy should be considered to maximize extent of resection while preserving motor function.
recommended_actions: Consider awake craniotomy with intraoperative cortical stimulation mapping
DTI tractography integration into surgical planning
Pre-operative discussion with patient regarding risk of contralateral motor deficit

output.query: glioblastoma IDH-wildtype standard of care treatment guidelines 2024

results.title: Radiotherapy plus Concomitant and Adjuvant Temozolomide for Glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al.
results.key_finding: The Stupp protocol established the standard of care: maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent radiotherapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with daily temozolomide (75 mg/m2), then 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2, days 1-5 of each 28-day cycle). Median overall survival improved from 12.1 to 14.6 months.

results.title: Effect of Tumor-Treating Fields Plus Maintenance Temozolomide vs Maintenance Temozolomide Alone on Survival in Patients With Glioblastoma: A Randomized Clinical Trial (EF-14)
results.authors: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al.
results.key_finding: Addition of tumor-treating fields (TTFields) to maintenance temozolomide improved median overall survival to 20.9 months vs 16.0 months. TTFields is now considered part of the standard of care for newly diagnosed GBM.

results.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines: Central Nervous System Cancers, Version 2.2024
results.key_finding: Standard of care for newly diagnosed GBM: maximal safe resection, followed by RT (60 Gy) + concurrent TMZ, then adjuvant TMZ + TTFields. For MGMT unmethylated tumors, temozolomide benefit is reduced but still recommended. Clinical trial enrollment should be offered.

output.summary: The current standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma (IDH-wildtype) consists of maximal safe surgical resection followed by the Stupp protocol: concurrent chemoradiotherapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions with daily temozolomide 75 mg/m2) followed by 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2 days 1-5 of each 28-day cycle). Tumor-treating fields (TTFields) added to maintenance temozolomide has shown survival benefit and is now incorporated into standard therapy. For patients with unmethylated MGMT promoter, temozolomide benefit is diminished but still recommended per guidelines. Clinical trial enrollment should always be discussed. Median overall survival remains approximately 14-21 months with current best therapy.

interactions: Metformin: Dexamethasone causes significant hyperglycemia and can counteract the glucose-lowering effect of metformin. Blood glucose monitoring and insulin coverage will likely be needed.
Aspirin: Concurrent corticosteroids and aspirin increase the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding. Consider GI prophylaxis with a proton pump inhibitor.
Ibuprofen: Combined corticosteroid and NSAID use significantly increases the risk of GI ulceration and bleeding. Ibuprofen should be discontinued.
warnings: Monitor blood glucose closely; sliding-scale insulin coverage recommended given patient's diabetes
Administer with proton pump inhibitor for GI prophylaxis
Taper gradually when discontinuing to avoid adrenal crisis
Monitor for steroid-related neuropsychiatric effects including insomnia, agitation, and psychosis
alternatives: Methylprednisolone 16 mg IV every 6 hours (equivalent anti-inflammatory dose)
Mannitol 20% (0.5-1 g/kg IV) for acute ICP crisis if corticosteroids are insufficient

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV (high-grade glioma)

differential.key_distinguishing: Solitary lesion with infiltrative borders; metastases tend to be multiple, spherical, at the gray-white junction with thinner ring enhancement; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis negative for primary malignancy

No fever, normal WBC, no source of infection; abscess shows smooth thin ring enhancement with central restricted diffusion from pus; perfusion imaging shows low rCBV in abscess ring vs high rCBV in GBM
Lymphoma typically shows homogeneous enhancement without central necrosis, is often periventricular, and demonstrates dramatic response to corticosteroids; patient is immunocompetent
Open ring enhancement pattern, typically less mass effect for lesion size, younger patient demographic, often associated with MS history; spectroscopy would show less dramatic metabolite changes

optimal_actions.action: Perform initial assessment with complete neurological examination including fundoscopic exam to evaluate for papilledema and signs of elevated intracranial pressure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left-sided weakness, left homonymous hemianopia, word-finding difficulty, papilledema indicating elevated ICP

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, hepatic panel, and HbA1c as baseline for surgical and chemotherapy planning
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC and coagulation (surgical readiness), mildly elevated glucose from known diabetes, normal liver and kidney function

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast including DWI, FLAIR, and post-contrast sequences
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large right frontal heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis, ring enhancement, vasogenic edema, and midline shift consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone 10 mg IV loading dose then 4 mg IV every 6 hours for cerebral edema and elevated ICP, with GI prophylaxis and glucose monitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in headache, nausea, and neurological symptoms as edema decreases

optimal_actions.action: Start antiepileptic medication (levetiracetam 500 mg IV twice daily) given clinical seizure presentation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Seizure control; levetiracetam has minimal drug interactions and does not require hepatic enzyme induction monitoring

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizures and characterize epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Focal slowing and epileptiform discharges over the right frontotemporal region consistent with structural lesion and peri-tumoral cortical irritability

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast to rule out primary systemic malignancy (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of primary systemic malignancy, supporting diagnosis of primary brain tumor

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurosurgery for maximal safe resection versus stereotactic biopsy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Neurosurgical plan for craniotomy with maximal safe resection; consideration of awake craniotomy given proximity to motor cortex

optimal_actions.action: Obtain functional MRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left-lateralized language function (contralateral to tumor); left hand motor area adjacent to tumor margin requiring careful surgical planning

optimal_actions.action: Proceed with maximal safe surgical resection with tissue sent for histopathology and molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q, TERT, EGFR)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Tissue diagnosis of glioblastoma IDH-wildtype with molecular characterization to guide adjuvant therapy

optimal_actions.action: After recovery from surgery, initiate Stupp protocol: concurrent radiation therapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with daily temozolomide (75 mg/m2), followed by adjuvant temozolomide and tumor-treating fields
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard of care adjuvant treatment initiated within 3-6 weeks of surgery

optimal_actions.action: Discuss prognosis, goals of care, advanced directives, and consideration of clinical trial enrollment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Informed patient and family; appropriate palliative care involvement

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to identify and characterize the mass lesion
Initiate dexamethasone to manage cerebral edema and elevated intracranial pressure
Start antiepileptic medication given clinical seizure
Urgent neurosurgical consultation for surgical planning
Obtain tissue diagnosis via resection or biopsy with molecular profiling
Rule out brain metastasis with systemic cancer workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis)
Plan for Stupp protocol (concurrent chemoradiotherapy with temozolomide) following surgical resection
contraindicated_actions: Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of large intracranial mass with significant midline shift and signs of elevated ICP (risk of brain herniation)
Administering phenytoin or carbamazepine for seizure control (enzyme-inducing AEDs interact with chemotherapy and corticosteroids; levetiracetam is preferred)
Withholding corticosteroids in the presence of significant edema and mass effect with early herniation
Delaying neurosurgical consultation in the setting of progressive neurological decline and midline shift
Starting empiric antibiotics for presumed brain abscess without tissue diagnosis when imaging strongly suggests high-grade glioma
Performing whole-brain radiation therapy without tissue diagnosis
key_reasoning_points: Progressive neurological symptoms (headaches worse in the morning, nausea/vomiting, focal weakness, cognitive decline) over weeks in a 61-year-old man are highly suspicious for an intracranial mass lesion
New-onset seizure in an adult without prior seizure history mandates brain imaging to evaluate for structural pathology
MRI findings of a large heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, extensive vasogenic edema, and midline shift are classic for glioblastoma
Papilledema on fundoscopic exam confirms elevated intracranial pressure and necessitates urgent intervention with corticosteroids
Right frontal location explains the left-sided motor deficits (contralateral) and word-finding difficulty (supplementary motor area and frontal language networks)
Dexamethasone is the first-line treatment for tumor-associated cerebral edema, but glucose must be monitored closely in a diabetic patient
EEG showing focal slowing with epileptiform discharges in the right frontotemporal region is consistent with the MRI-identified structural lesion, demonstrating cross-modal consistency
Maximal safe resection improves survival in glioblastoma and provides tissue for molecular profiling, which guides adjuvant treatment decisions

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - classic presentation with progressive headaches, focal neurological deficits, new-onset seizure, and MRI showing a large ring-enhancing mass with central necrosis and midline shift
clinical_pearls: New-onset seizure in an adult older than 40 should always prompt brain imaging to rule out structural pathology, particularly brain tumors
Morning headaches with nausea and vomiting suggest elevated intracranial pressure, a red flag for intracranial mass lesions
Glioblastoma is the most common and aggressive primary malignant brain tumor in adults, with a median survival of 14-21 months with optimal therapy
The Stupp protocol (surgery + concurrent chemoradiotherapy + adjuvant temozolomide) remains the standard of care since 2005
MGMT promoter methylation status is the most important molecular predictor of temozolomide response, but treatment is given regardless of methylation status
